# X12 EDI Connector - Full Documentation

# X12 EDI Connector 2.18

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Support Category:
[Premium](https://www.salesforce.com/en-us/wp-content/uploads/sites/4/documents/legal/Agreements/versioning-back-support-policy.pdf)

Anypoint Connector for X12 EDI (X12 EDI Connector) enables you to convert X12 messages to and from
DataWeave-compatible representations by using lists and maps. All of the schema definitions you need
for [supported X12 standard transaction sets](x12-edi-versions-x12) are included. For complete
information about schemas, see
[X12 EDI Schema Language Reference](x12-edi-schema-language-reference).

Many Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) document versions are also
supported, as listed separately in the [Supported HIPAA Versions](x12-edi-versions-hipaa). For more
information about HIPAA usage, refer to [Connector HIPAA Standards](x12-edi-connector-hipaa).

## Prerequisites

To use this connector, you must be familiar with:

- Anypoint Connectors
- Mule runtime engine (Mule)
- Elements and global elements in a Mule flow
- Creating a Mule app using Anypoint Studio or Anypoint Code Builder

Before creating an app, you must have:

- Purchased a MuleSoft license for X12 EDI Connector.

  Contact your Account Representative about purchasing a license.

- Anypoint Studio or Anypoint Code Builder

> [!NOTE] X12 EDI Connector supports files of up to 15 MB in size, and the memory requirement is
> approximately 40 to 1. For example, a 1 MB file requires up to 40 MB of memory to process, so itâs
> important to consider this memory requirement in conjunction with your Transactions Per Second
> (TPS) needs for large X12 files. This is not an exact figure; the value varies according to the
> complexity of the mapping instructions.

## Exchange Templates and Examples

[Anypoint Exchange](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/) provides templates that you can use as
starting points for your apps and examples that illustrate a complete solution.

### X12 EDI Connector Templates in Exchange

X12 EDI Connector templates provide starting points that help you:

- [Map application messages to outbound X12 transactions](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/org.mule.examples/template-b2b-edi-outbound-x12-map/minor/1.0/)
- [Map inbound X12 transactions to the application message format](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/org.mule.examples/template-b2b-edi-inbound-x12-map/minor/1.0/)
- [Set up your Partner Manager content storage](https://www.anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/com.mulesoft.b2b/partner-manager-content-storage-service-s3/minor/1.0/)

### X12 EDI Connector Examples in Exchange

X12 EDI Connector examples in Exchange enable you to:

- [Customize an X12 EDI schema for B2B integrations](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/works.integration/b2b-x12-custom-schema-example/minor/1.0/)
- [Transform outbound purchase order XML messages to X12 850 transactions](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/org.mule.examples/example-b2b-nto-outbound-purchase-order-01/minor/1.0/)
- [Transform inbound X12 850 transactions to JSON purchase orders](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/org.mule.examples/example-b2b-mythical-inbound-purchase-order-01/minor/1.0/)
- [Transform outbound XML messages into X12 purchase orders, inbound X12 purchase orders into JSON, and generate a functional acknowledgement](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/org.mule.examples/b2b-demo-x12-transform/)
- [Implement an end-to-end order to cash workflow from the perspective of a seller](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/com.mulesoft.muleesb.modules/b2b-order-to-cash-mapping/minor/1.0/)
- [Implement an end-to-end B2B procure to pay workflow from the perspective of a buyer](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/com.mulesoft.muleesb.modules/b2b-procure-to-pay-mappings/minor/1.0/)

## Next Step

After you complete the prerequisites, you are ready to create your own app and configure the
connector using [Anypoint Studio](x12-edi-connector-studio).

## See Also

[Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)

[Use Exchange to Discover Connectors, Templates, and Examples](../../connectors/introduction/intro-use-exchange)

[Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# X12 EDI Connector 2.18 Batch Outbound X12 Messages

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

For EDI messages that are not time-sensitive, such as invoice and payment remittances, use the Write
batch operation to merge multiple individual messages into a single interchange to send to your
trading partners.

## Batching Stages

Use the Anypoint Connector for X12 EDI (X12 EDI Connector) Write batch operation to merge individual
X12 messages into a single transmission to send to your trading partners.

Implementing a batch involves two stages:

1.  Accumulate the transactions.
2.  Release the batch.

### Accumulate the Transactions

To accumulate transactions for a batch:

1.  Transform the payloads into X12 messages using DataWeave and X12 as you receive application
    messages (such as a JSON invoice) from the backend systems.
2.  Create X12 payloads using temporary control number keys in the X12 Write operation.

    This enables sending the accumulated X12 as a batch at a later time. The batch is created using
    control numbers that are not used in the final batch that is sent to the trading partner, for
    example,
    `<x12:write doc:name="Create a single X12 810 Invoice Txn" doc:id="767e1321-044d-4ed4-95ba-6ddc78efb0b4" config-ref="X12_EDI_Write_810" interchangeNumberKey="MYTHICAL-NTOETAIL-ISA-TMP" groupNumberKey="MYTHICAL-NTOETAIL-GS-TMP"/>`.

3.  Store the intermediate X12 message payloads in your choice of storage, such as a database table:

![Accumulate the transactions](_images/X12-edi-connector-batch-flow-1.jpg)

For example, this implementation of a Mule flow:

- Receives JSON payloads from the backend systems.
- Transforms to an `X12 810` invoice message.
- Generates the intermediate X12 message payload using the X12 Write operation.
- Stores the X12 payload in a database table.

### Release the Batch

Trading partners have varying criteria for how they want transactions batched:

- Release the batch at a specific time interval or a specific time of the day.
- Release the batch when a specific number of transactions are accumulated.
- Release the batch when the total size of the accumulated transactions is near a specified
  threshold.

To release the batch:

1.  Implement the logic or module to monitor the transactions accumulated in the temporary storage
    and determine when a particular batch is released.
2.  Retrieve the accumulated intermediate X12 messages from the temporary storage based on the batch
    criteria, group the transactions together, and then send the appended payload to the X12 Write
    batch operation.

    You can either use the control number key on the Write batch operation or leave the control
    number key fields empty at the time of Write batch operation execution, allowing the X12 EDI
    Connector to generate the control number sequence automatically based on the sender and receiver
    identifier combination:

    `<x12:batch doc:name="Batch" doc:id="6f105235-1406-45bb-b41e-f3b28cdf2eb5" config-ref="X12_EDI_Batch" batchInterchangeNumberKey="MYTHICAL-NTOETAIL-ISA" batchGroupNumberKey="MYTHICAL-NTOETAIL-GS"> <x12:batch-content ><![CDATA[#[vars.batchInput]]]></x12:batch-content></x12:batch>`.

3.  Ensure that the input payload sent to the Write batch operation contains EDI transactions with
    data elements in the ISA and GS envelope segments, such as `Sender ID`, `Receiver ID`, and
    `Version number`:

![Release the batch](_images/x12-edi-connector-batch-flow-2.png)

For example, this implementation of a Mule flow:

- Receives an HTTP request to release accumulated X12 messages transactions for a specific partner.
  The request is triggered after the batch release criteria are met.
- Retrieves the intermediate X12 messages from the database table.
- Applies a transformation to group the individual X12 messages.
- Sends the appended payload through the X12 Write batch operation to merge the messages into a
  single transaction.
- Prints the generated control numbers, which reconcile the inbound functional acknowledgments
  received from the partners.
- Delivers the batched X12 transaction to the trading partnerâs SFTP site.

If the input payload contains EDI transactions with non-matching data elements in the envelope
segments, the Batch operation throws an exception.
---

# X12 EDI Connector 2.18 Schema and Trading Partner Configuration

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Use these additional XML configuration options to:

- Set your schema location if you are using custom schemas
- Define your trading partner relationship by setting SELF or PARTNER identification

You can set the types of error conditions that reject receive transaction sets and any writer
message separator operations. These general parameters control both Send and Receive document
handling.

## Set Your Schema Locations

If youâre using one or more custom schemas, locate these under a directory in `src/main/resources`
and refer to the location relative to this directory. For example, if an `850` schema (either
overlay or full) is under `src/main/resources/mypartner/850.esl`, the schema location is
`/mypartner/850.esl`.

You can configure schema locations in the Anypoint Studio XML view. The schema files are loaded when
the connector is initialized, so runtime expressions such as flow variables are not supported.

To configure schemas in Anypoint Studio:

1.  Switch to the XML view by clicking Configuration XML
2.  Modify your X12 EDI configuration to include a list of all the schemas for use by adding an
    `<x12:schema>` element for each document type:

```xml
<x12-edi:config name="X12_EDI__Configuration" identKeys="true" doc:name="X12 EDI: Configuration">
  <x12-edi:schemas>
    <x12:schema value="/x12/005010/850.esl"/>
  </x12-edi:schemas>
</x12-edi:config>
```

If you donât include any schemas in the configuration, X12 EDI Connector attempts to load the
standard X12 schema definitions provided with the connector. However, in this case you wonât see any
metadata to define the structure of the data passed in and out of the connector.

If the schema definition configuration includes a `997` or `999` Functional Acknowledgment schema,
then that schema is used to generate Functional Acknowledgments.

> [!NOTE] To use the Functional Acknowledgment schema type (`997` or `999`), that schema must match
> the `generate999Acks` Parser parameter (`false` for `997`, `true` for `999`).

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>XML Value (default)</th><th>Studio Option</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>formValidation="X12"/"HIPAA_SNIP1"/"HIPAA_SNIP2"</p></td><td><p>The schema form and validation level can be either X12 or HIPAA. The default is X12.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>schemas=list of values</p></td><td><p>List of schema paths the connector can use. These paths can be either a file system or <code>classpath</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>characterEncoding="PLATFORM"/"ASCII"/"ISO8859_1"/"IBM1047"/"UTF8"</p></td><td><p>The character encoding used for both send and receive messages. The default <code>PLATFORM</code> is the Mule default encoding.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>stringCharacterSet="BASIC"/"EXTENDED"/"UNRESTRICTED"</p></td><td><p>Use a substitution character for invalid characters, or reject them as errors if set to <code>none</code> or enabled for receive messages in the parser options. The default is <code>EXTENDED</code> for string data.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>stringSubstitutionChar="?"</p></td><td><p>Substitution character used to replace invalid characters in string values. The default is <code>none</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>versionIdentifierSuffix="XYZ"</p></td><td><p>Version identifier code suffix for the group (GS). Setting this value affects both Send and Receive message handling. On the Receive side, the GS08 version code must contain X12 schema version (for example, <code>004010</code>, <code>005010</code>, and so on) followed by this suffix. Not setting this value means the Receive-only just verifies that the GS version identifier code starts with the configured schema version. On the Send side, the combination of X12 schema version and this suffix comprises the GS08 value.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>lockMechanism="AUTO"/"RUNTIME_LOCK_MANAGER"/"OBJECT_STORE"</p></td><td><p>Mechanism to use to manage locks for control number sequencing.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Self Identification Parameters

Use these parameters to identify your side of the trading partner relationship.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>XML Value (default)</th><th>Studio Option</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>interchangeIdQualifierSelf="ZZ"</p></td><td><p>The ID qualifier for Mule in Exchange (known as the <em>ISA segment</em>). Use this value in combination with the <code>Interchange ID</code> value. Both values must be either specified or not specified.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>interchangeIdSelf="MULE"</p></td><td><p>The ID qualifier for Mule runtime engine in Exchange (known as the ISA segment). Use this value in combination with the <code>Interchange ID</code> value. Both values must be either specified or not specified.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>groupIdSelf="MULEAPP"</p></td><td><p>Application code for Mule runtime engine in a group (GS segment).</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Partner Identification Parameters

These identify your partner.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>XML Value (default)</th><th>Studio Option</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>interchangeIdQualifierSelf="ZZ"</p></td><td><p>The ID qualifier for a partner in Exchange (known as the <em>ISA segment</em>). Use this value in combination with the <code>Interchange ID</code> value. Both values must be either specified or not specified.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>interchangeIdSelf="PARTNER"</p></td><td><p>The ID qualifier for a partner in Exchange (known as the <em>ISA segment</em>). Use this value in combination with the <code>Interchange ID</code> value. Both values must be either specified or not specified.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>groupIdSelf="PARTNERAPP"</p></td><td><p>Application Code for a partner in a group (GS segment)</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Parser Parameters for Error Conditions

These control the parser operation and the types of error conditions that reject Receive transaction
sets.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>XML Value (default)</th><th>Studio Option</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>enforceLengthLimits="true"</p></td><td><p>Enforce the minimum and maximum lengths for receive values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>truncateExceedingMaxLength="false"</p></td><td><p>Truncate to the maximum lengths for received values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceCharacterSet="true"</p></td><td><p>Enforce the allowed character set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceValueRepeats="true"</p></td><td><p>Enforce the repetition count limits for receive values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>allowUnknownSegments="false"</p></td><td><p>Allow unknown segments in a transaction set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceSegmentOrder="true"</p></td><td><p>Enforce the segment order in a transaction set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>allowUnusedSegments="false"</p></td><td><p>Allow segments marked as <code>Unused</code> in a transaction set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceSegmentRepeats="true"</p></td><td><p>Enforce segment repetition count limits in a transaction set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>requireUniqueInterchanges="true"</p></td><td><p>Require unique ISA Interchange Control Numbers (such as ISA13). The default behavior records the interchange numbers previously processed and rejects duplicate interchange numbers from the same partner. It is derived from the interchange Sender and Receiver identification. Setting to <code>false</code> enables received interchange processing to continue and relies on the application flow to track the numbers and handle them appropriately.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>requireUniqueGroups="false"</p></td><td><p>Enforce globally unique Group Control Numbers (GS06) for received functional groups. By default, group numbers need to be unique within an interchange. Setting to <code>true</code> requires group numbers to be unique across all interchanges received from the same partner and application. This is derived from the interchange sender and receiver identification combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>requireUniqueTransactionSets="false"</p></td><td><p>Enforce globally unique Transaction Set Control Numbers (ST02) for received transaction sets. By default, transaction set control numbers need to be unique only within a particular functional group. Setting to <code>true</code> requires transaction set numbers to be unique across all functional groups received from the same partner and application. This is derived from the interchange sender and receiver identification, combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>daysToStore="30"</p></td><td><p>Minimum number of days to store interchange, group, and transaction set numbers for uniqueness checking.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>ackAllSets="false"</p></td><td><p>Include a separate AK2/AK5 (<code>997</code>) or AK2/IK5 (<code>999</code>) acknowledgment for every Received transaction set. By default (<code>false</code>), transaction sets containing errors are part of the acknowledgment, with all other transaction sets implicitly acknowledged. Changing to <code>true</code> acknowledges each Received transaction set.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>generate999Acks="false"</p></td><td><p>Generate <code>999</code> Implementation Acknowledgments instead of <code>997</code> Functional Acknowledgments. Setting to <code>false</code>, the <code>997</code> Functional Acknowledgment transaction sets are generated for each received interchange. Setting to <code>true</code> generates <code>999</code> Implementation Acknowledgments instead. Support for <code>999</code> Implementation Acknowledgments does not include CTX segment generation.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>reportSegmentErrors="true"</p></td><td><p>Report segment error details to the sender in the <code>997`or`999</code> flag. Setting to <code>true</code> includes the details of any segment errors that are generated in the <code>997`or`999</code> flag. Setting to <code>false</code> does not include the details.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>includeFASchema="true"</p></td><td><p>Expect <code>997</code> or <code>999</code> Functional Acknowledgments and include the <code>997</code> or <code>999</code> schema. <code>true</code> automatically includes the schema for the <code>997</code> or <code>999</code> acknowledgment transaction sets within the set of schemas used by X12 EDI connector. Setting to <code>false</code>, means directly specifying the <code>997</code> or <code>999</code> schema to process as the input. The schemas used for generating <code>997</code> or <code>999</code> Functional Acknowledgments are hardcoded and cannot be modified.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>setAckSchemaByInboundMessage="false"</p></td><td><p>If the <strong>acknowledgmentSchemaPath</strong> is <code>EMPTY</code> and this property is <code>true</code> it will use the <strong>Version / Release / Industry Identifier Code (GS08)</strong> field value from the inbound message to set the Functional/Implementation Acknowledgment structure. When <code>false</code> it uses a hardcoded Functional/Implementation Acknowledgment structure of the 005010 version.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>acknowledgmentSchemaPath=""</p></td><td><p>Expect the path either from the file system or the <code>classpath</code>. If the path is specified, it overwrites the default acknowledgment path.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceConditionalRules="false"</p></td><td><p>Enforce conditional rules for Receive values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceCodeSetValidationsParse="false"</p></td><td><p>Enforce code set validations on the <strong>Read</strong> operation.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>reportWarningMessagesInACK="true"</p></td><td><p>Report non-fatal errors in acknowledgments.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>includeTrailerSegments="false"</p></td><td><p>Include 'InterchangeTrailer' and 'GroupTrailer' in each transaction</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>includeElementsFromIgnoredSegments="false"</p></td><td><p>Whether to include data elements from segments with Ignore as the usage type in the output map and metadata.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Writer Parameters

These control the types of writer operations.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>XML Value (default)</th><th>Studio Option</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>dataSeparator="*"</p></td><td><p>Data element separator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>componentSeparator="&gt;"</p></td><td><p>Component separator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>repetitionSeparator="U"</p></td><td><p>Repetition separator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level. <code>U</code> means repetitions are not used</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>segmentTerminator="~"</p></td><td><p>Segment terminator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>lineEnding="NONE"/"LF"/"CRLF"/"CR"</p></td><td><p>Line ending to add between segments. The default is <code>NONE</code>. You can add line endings between segments to improve message text output readability.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>writeUseCRLFLastLine="false"</p></td><td><p>Use configured 'Segment Line Ending' at the end of the last line.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>sendUniqueGroupNumbers="false"</p></td><td><p>Send unique Group Control Numbers. <code>false</code> (default) assigns functional group control numbers sequentially within each interchange and reuses them in different interchanges. <code>true</code> assigns unique group numbers across all interchanges sent to the same partner and application. It is derived from the interchange sender and receiver identification combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>sendUniqueTransactionNumbers="false"</p></td><td><p>Send unique Transaction Set Control Numbers. <code>false</code> (default) assigns transaction set control numbers sequentially within each functional group and reuses them in different groups. <code>true</code> assigns unique transaction set numbers across all interchanges sent to the same partner and application. It is derived from the interchange sender and receiver identification, combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>implementationConventionReference=""</p></td><td><p>Implementation convention reference for transactions (ST segment). Setting this value uses the ST Implementation Convention Reference unless overridden in the message parameters.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>initialInterchangeNumber="1"</p></td><td><p>The initial Interchange Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>initialGroupNumber="1"</p></td><td><p>The initial Group Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>initialSetNumber="1"</p></td><td><p>The initial Transaction Set Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>ackRequested="false"</p></td><td><p>Request acknowledgments for sent transactions flag. If true, <code>997</code> or <code>999</code> acknowledgments are requested for all sent transactions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>defaultUsageIndicator="P"</p></td><td><p>Default ISA15 interchange usage indicator: <code>I</code> for Information, <code>P</code> for Production Data, <code>T</code> for Test Data.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>useSuppliedValues="false"</p></td><td><p>Use values from supplied data for control segment identifiers (ISA/IEA, GS/GE, ST/SE segments). <code>false</code> generates control numbers when writing.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>outputEdiMimeType="APPLICATION_PLAIN"/"APPLICATION_EDIX12"</p></td><td><p>Output MIME type to be set for the message, either the default <code>application/plain</code> or X12-specific alternative <code>application/edi-x12</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>writeEnforceLengthLimits="true"</p></td><td><p>Enforce minimum and maximum lengths for write values. The default of <code>true</code> throws an exception when an element is too long or too short. <code>false</code> leaves the values as-is.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>writeTruncateExceedingMaxLength="false"</p></td><td><p>Truncate to the maximum lengths for received values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceConditionalRulesOnWriter="false"</p></td><td><p>Enforce conditional rules for write values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>interchangeNumberKey=""</p></td><td><p>Interchange number key name for the object store.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceCodeSetValidationsWrite="false"</p></td><td><p>Enforce code set validations on the <strong>Write</strong> operation.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Write Batch Parameters

These control the types of Write batch (`<edifact:write-batch>`) operations.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>XML Value (default)</th><th>Studio Option</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>batchDataSeparator="*"</p></td><td><p>Data element separator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchComponentSeparator="&gt;"</p></td><td><p>Component separator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchRepetitionSeparator="U"</p></td><td><p>Repetition separator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level. <code>U</code> means repetitions are not used</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchSegmentTerminator="~"</p></td><td><p>Segment terminator character. The default uses the configured value for all output messages and can be overridden at the message level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchLineEnding="NONE"/"LF"/"CRLF"/"CR"</p></td><td><p>Line ending to add between segments. The default is <code>NONE</code>. You can add line endings between segments to improve message text output readability.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchUseCRLFLastLine="false"</p></td><td><p>Use configured 'Segment Line Ending' at the end of the last line.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchSendUniqueGroupNumbers="false"</p></td><td><p>Send unique Group Control Numbers. <code>false</code> (default) assigns functional group control numbers sequentially within each interchange and reuses them in different interchanges. <code>true</code> assigns unique group numbers across all interchanges sent to the same partner and application. It is derived from the interchange sender and receiver identification combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchSendUniqueTransactionNumbers="false"</p></td><td><p>Send unique Transaction Set Control Numbers. <code>false</code> (default) assigns transaction set control numbers sequentially within each functional group and reuses them in different groups. <code>true</code> assigns unique transaction set numbers across all interchanges sent to the same partner and application. It is derived from the interchange sender and receiver identification, combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchInitialInterchangeNumber="1"</p></td><td><p>The initial Interchange Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchInitialGroupNumber="1"</p></td><td><p>The initial Group Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchInitialSetNumber="1"</p></td><td><p>The initial Transaction Set Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchAckRequested="false"</p></td><td><p>Request acknowledgments for sent transactions flag. If true, <code>997</code> or <code>999</code> acknowledgments are requested for all sent transactions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchDefaultUsageIndicator="P"</p></td><td><p>Default ISA15 interchange usage indicator: <code>I</code> for Information, <code>P</code> for Production Data, <code>T</code> for Test Data.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchOutputEdiMimeType="APPLICATION_PLAIN"/"APPLICATION_EDIX12"</p></td><td><p>Output MIME type to be set for the message, either the default <code>application/plain</code> or X12-specific alternative <code>application/edi-x12</code>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchInterchangeNumberKey=""</p></td><td><p>Interchange number key name for object store.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchGroupNumberKey=""</p></td><td><p>Group number key name for object store.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchTransactionNumberKey=""</p></td><td><p>Transaction number key name for object store.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchEnforceLengthLimits="TRUE"</p></td><td><p>Enforce minimum and maximum lengths for received values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>batchTruncateExceedingMaxLength="false"</p></td><td><p>Truncate to the maximum lengths for received values.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>enforceCodeSetValidationsBatch="false"</p></td><td><p>Enforce code set validations on the <strong>Write Batch</strong> operation.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Next Step

After you complete configuring the connector, you can try the
[Examples](x12-edi-connector-examples).

## See Also

- [Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# X12 EDI Connector 2.18 Control Numbers

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Using the Write operation in the Anypoint Connector for X12 EDI Connector (X12 EDI Connector), you
can generate control numbers on the generated X12 payload by:

- Managing global control numbers through control number keys
- Managing identifier combination control numbers
- Using your own control number

## Manage Global Control Numbers Through Control Number Keys

If your organization or your trading partner have must maintain unique interchange, group, or
transaction set control numbers across different identifier combinations and document types:

- Set the configuration `Use supplied values` to `false`.
- Leverage the control number key configurations (at the configuration level or operation level) to
  set a key unique to the partner.

  By default, the control number key configurations pass dynamically at the operation level. You can
  then use this group control number key across different identifiers and transaction types for the
  same partner.

![X12 EDI Connector Control Numbers](_images/X12-edi-control-number.jpg)

## Manage Identifier Combination Control Numbers

If you want the connector to automatically maintain control number sequencing based on the
sender/receiver identifiers and you do not require global control numbers at a partner level, then
set the Write configuration `Use supplied values` to `false`. When no value passes into the optional
interchange, group, or transaction number key configurations, the sender/receiver identifier
combination is the default.

## Use Your Own Control Number

If you must generate the control numbers and use them on the EDI payload, you can set the parameter
`Use supplied values` to `true`, and map the control number on the payload in the DataWeave map.
---

# X12 EDI Connector 2.18 Examples

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Use this example to view the use case for Business-to-Business (B2B) EDI integration leveraging
Anypoint X12 Connector. This example:

- Transforms XML messages into outbound EDI X12 850 purchase order transactions

An HTTPS Listener source receives the enterprise purchase order XML messages from a backend system.
DataWeave and X12 Writes translate the XML message into an X12 v4010 850 purchase order EDI message.

- Transforms Inbound EDI X12 850 purchase orders into JSON

An HTTP Listener source receives the raw EDI X12 850 purchase order message. X12 Read and DataWeave
components pare and translate the EDI data into the JSON format.

- Generates a Functional Acknowledgment (997)

## Prerequisites

1.  Open the
    [Transform XML to EDI X12 850 purchase orders and X12 850 to JSON](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/org.mule.examples/b2b-demo-x12-transform/)
    example in Anypoint Studio from
    [Anypoint Exchange Examples](https://www.mulesoft.com/exchange/org.mule.examples/).
2.  Install the latest version of Anypoint X12 Connector.

## Run and Test the Outbound XML to EDI X12 850

1.  Run the application in Anypoint Studio.
2.  Test the outbound flow by using a REST Client to post a request (POST) to a JSON message to the
    URL:

`[http://localhost:8081/x12/send/enterprise-ob-purchaseorder](http://localhost:8081/x12/send/enterprise-ob-purchaseorder)`

You see the payload location under the following directory:

`src/main/resources/outbound/Enterprise-Outbound-PO.xml`

The Mule app returns the generated EDI X12 850 transaction.

## Run and Test the Inbound EDI X12 850 to JSON

1.  Test the inbound flow using a REST Client to post a request (POST) to a raw EDI X12 850 message
    to the URL:

    `[http://localhost:8081/x12/receive/4010-850](http://localhost:8081/x12/receive/4010-850)`

    You see the payload location under the following directory:

    `src/main/resources/inbound/EDI-4010-850-NTO-to-Mythical.edi`

    The app returns the translated JSON purchase order message.

    A Functional Acknowledgement (997) is generated and sent to the log.

2.  Send the message back to the trading partner via any standard protocol, such as FTP, SFTP, or
    AS2.

## See Also

- [Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# X12 EDI Connector 2.18 HIPAA

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Connector for X12 EDI Connector 2.9 - HIPAA provides support for the Health Insurance
Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) standards based on X12 EDI Connector standards and
version `005010` document type. This information explains how to use X12 EDI Connector for HIPAA
transaction sets, and for how they differ from the standard X12. For complete information about X12
standards, refer to [X12 EDI Connector Overview](./) and
[EDI Schema Language Reference](x12-edi-schema-language-reference).

X12 EDI Connector - HIPAA support includes:

- X12 HIPAA EDI message reading, message validation, and message writing
- Integration with DataSense and DataWeave
- The ability to customize the base X12 HIPAA schemas

Most aspects of using HIPAA with X12 EDI Connector are the same as using standard X12. The main
differences are:

- The form of the schemas
- The extent and type of validation performed
- The data structure representation

The supplied HIPAA schemas use different structures from the base X12 schemas. The structure used
for HIPAA reflects the way that the same segments are used in different locations in a transaction
set for different purposes, and often with different requirements for code sets and field usages. To
help distinguish between the different uses, the HIPAA schemas use full names as segment keys. This
differs from the approach used with basic X12 standards, in which the segment keys normally use a
numeric prefix along with the segment tag.

HIPAA schemas also include details of code sets defined for elements. Data is validated against
these code sets both when parsing and writing transaction sets.

## Configuring for HIPAA Usage

To use X12 EDI Connector for HIPAA transaction sets you must:

- Select one or more HIPAA schemas to be used.
- Set the Form and Validation configuration parameter (`formValidation`) to `HIPAA_SNIP1` or
  `HIPAA_SNIP2`, depending on the type of desired SNIP validation.

### Determining HIPAA Schema Locations

To use the connector, you need to know the locations of the schemas in your project. If youâre using
the out-of-the-box HIPAA schemas and not customizing anything, the schema location follows the
`/hipaa/{version}/{transaction-set}.esl` convention. For example, if youâre using the `005010X222A2`
version and the `837` transaction set, your schema location is `/hipaa/005010X222A2/837.esl`.

If youâre using one or more custom schemas, place these under `src/main/resources` and refer to the
location relative to this directory. For example, if youâve put your HIPAA `837` schema under
`src/main/app/mypartner/837.esl`, your schema location is `/mypartner/837.esl`.

## Differences in HIPAA Schema Definitions

X12 EDI Connector uses a YAML format called EDI Schema Language (ESL) to represent both standard X12
and HIPAA schemas. The HIPAA standards are based on X12 but modify the corresponding X12 base
standards in several aspects, including:

- Multiple specialized definitions of Hierarchical Level (HL) loops. Where a basic loop structure
  defined in the X12 structure is expanded into several variations. These loops are often nested.
- Modular loop definitions that can be reused at different points in the definition.
- Distinguishing between different uses of a repeated segment by code values in a particular element
- Changing usage requirements for segments and segment components

These features of the HIPAA ESL schema definitions are very different from standard X12 schemas

This partial example illustrates the differences. It is from the schema for the `005010X222A2` `837`
transaction set:

```yaml
form: HIPAA
version: "005010X222A2"
structures:
  - id: "837"
    name: "Health Care Claim"
    class: "HC"
    areas:
      - code: "1"
        items:
          - { idRef: "ST_TransactionSetHeader", position: "0050", usage: M }
          - { idRef: "BHT_BeginningOfHierarchicalTransaction", position: "0100", usage: M }
          - groupId: "1000A_Loop"
            usage: O
            items:
              - { idRef: "NM1_SubmitterName", position: "0200", usage: M }
              - {
                  idRef: "PER_SubmitterEDIContactInformation",
                  position: "0450",
                  usage: M,
                  count: 2,
                }
          - groupId: "1000B_Loop"
            usage: O
            items:
              - { idRef: "NM1_ReceiverName", position: "0500", usage: M }
      - code: "2"
        items:
          - groupId: "2000A_Loop"
            count: ">1"
            usage: M
            items:
              - { idRef: "HL_BillingProviderHierarchicalLevel", position: "0010", usage: I }
              - { idRef: "PRV_BillingProviderSpecialtyInformation", position: "0030", usage: O }
              - { idRef: "CUR_ForeignCurrencyInformation", position: "0100", usage: O }
              - groupId: "2010AA_Loop"
                usage: O
                items:
                  - { idRef: "NM1_BillingProviderName", position: "0150", usage: M }
                  - { idRef: "N3_BillingProviderAddress", position: "0250", usage: M }
                  - { idRef: "N4_BillingProviderCityStateZIPCode", position: "0300", usage: M }
                  - { idRef: "REF_BillingProviderTaxIdentification", position: "0350", usage: M }
                  - {
                      idRef: "REF_BillingProviderUPINLicenseInformation",
                      position: "0360",
                      usage: O,
                      count: 2,
                    }
                  - {
                      idRef: "PER_BillingProviderContactInformation",
                      position: "0400",
                      usage: O,
                      count: 2,
                    }
              - { area: "3", usage: O }
              - { area: "4", count: ">1", usage: O }
      - code: "3"
        items:
          - groupId: "2010AB_Loop"
            usage: O
            items:
              - { idRef: "NM1_PayToAddressName", position: "0150", usage: O }
              - { idRef: "N3_PayToAddressADDRESS", position: "0250", usage: M }
              - { idRef: "N4_PayToAddressCityStateZIPCode", position: "0300", usage: M }
          - groupId: "2010AC_Loop"
            usage: O
            items:
              - { idRef: "NM1_PayToPlanName", position: "0450", usage: O }
              - { idRef: "N3_PayToPlanAddress", position: "0550", usage: M }
              - { idRef: "N4_PayToPlanCityStateZIPCode", position: "0600", usage: M }
              - { idRef: "REF_PayToPlanSecondaryIdentification", position: "0650", usage: O }
              - { idRef: "REF_PayToPlanTaxIdentificationNumber", position: "0655", usage: M }
```

## Using the Areas Key

In the previous schema example, the areas key has a value array of individual area definitions.
Areas are similar to the breakdown of normal X12 transaction sets, for example into header, detail,
and summary sections, but provide much finer granularity. Instead of the three fixed portions of a
transaction set "as defined in X12" there may be twenty or more areas defined in a HIPAA transaction
set.

Each area is a reusable component of the definition, and is identified by a code character value,
which by convention can be a single digit or single alpha character.

Areas are referenced for inclusion in the definition with an area item in the component list. In an
X12 schema definition, the list of components of a group or area can contain only segments, groups,
and a group variation called wrapped (used for LS/LE loops, in X12 terms). In a HIPAA schema
definition, the list of components can also contain area references. The effect of referencing an
area is the same as if all the components of the area were inserted in the definition at the point
of the reference.

Referring back to the example schema piece, the end of the components list for area code `2` are
references to areas `3` and `4`, with area `4` optionally repeating.

The data structure for HIPAA messages maintains the X12 division into Heading, Detail, and Summary
sections. The Heading is always the area with the lowest sort order code, the Detail is next
(including all referenced areas), and the Summary is the highest sort order code.

## Using Code Sets

The following is another portion of the same `005010X222A2` `837` transaction set schema example,
but this portion shows the `BHT_BeginningOfHierarchicalTransaction` segment definition:

```yaml
- id: "BHT_BeginningOfHierarchicalTransaction"
  name: "Beginning of Hierarchical Transaction"
  varTag: "BHT"
  values:
    - {
        id: "1005",
        name: "Hierarchical Structure Code",
        usage: M,
        codeSet: { "0019": "Information Source, Subscriber, Dependent" },
        type: ID,
        length: 4,
      }
    - {
        id: "353",
        name: "Transaction Set Purpose Code",
        usage: M,
        codeSet: { "00": "Original", "18": "Reissue" },
        type: ID,
        length: 2,
      }
    - {
        id: "127",
        name: "Originator Application Transaction Identifier",
        usage: M,
        type: AN,
        minLength: 1,
        maxLength: 50,
      }
    - { id: "373", name: "Transaction Set Creation Date", usage: M, type: DT, length: 8 }
    - {
        id: "337",
        name: "Transaction Set Creation Time",
        usage: M,
        type: TM,
        minLength: 4,
        maxLength: 8,
      }
    - {
        id: "640",
        name: "Claim or Encounter Identifier",
        usage: M,
        codeSet: { "RP": "Reporting", "CH": "Chargeable", "31": "Subrogation Demand" },
        type: ID,
        length: 2,
      }
```

The first, second, and last elements in this segment define `codeSet` values, in the form of an
array of key-value pairs. The key in each pair is a particular value for the field that is allowed
by the HIPAA standard, while the value in the pair is the text description of that value (from the
standard).

X12 EDI Connector enforces these code sets for HIPAA documents, signaling an error if a transaction
set uses an undefined value for a field. For example, a value not listed as a key in the `codeSet`
either when parsing or writing. In some cases, such as the first element of the `BHT` definition,
only a single value is allowed. In other cases there can be many potential values.

## Specifying Segment Variants

The following is a third portion of the same `005010X222A2` `837` transaction set schema example.
This example shows two different DTP segment definitions:

```yaml
- id: "DTP_DateAccident"
  name: "Date - Accident"
  varTag: "DTP"
  values:
    - {
        id: "374",
        name: "Date Time Qualifier",
        usage: M,
        varValue: true,
        codeSet: { "439": "Accident" },
        type: ID,
        length: 3,
      }
    - {
        id: "1250",
        name: "Date Time Period Format Qualifier",
        usage: M,
        codeSet: { "D8": "Date Expressed in Format CCYYMMDD" },
        type: ID,
        minLength: 2,
        maxLength: 3,
      }
    - { id: "1251", name: "Accident Date", usage: M, type: AN, minLength: 1, maxLength: 35 }
- id: "DTP_DateAcuteManifestation"
  name: "Date - Acute Manifestation"
  varTag: "DTP"
  values:
    - {
        id: "374",
        name: "Date Time Qualifier",
        usage: M,
        varValue: true,
        codeSet: { "453": "Acute Manifestation of a Chronic Condition" },
        type: ID,
        length: 3,
      }
    - {
        id: "1250",
        name: "Date Time Period Format Qualifier",
        usage: M,
        codeSet: { "D8": "Date Expressed in Format CCYYMMDD" },
        type: ID,
        minLength: 2,
        maxLength: 3,
      }
    - {
        id: "1251",
        name: "Acute Manifestation Date",
        usage: M,
        type: AN,
        minLength: 1,
        maxLength: 35,
      }
```

These two definitions apply to different instances of the `DTP` segment, as part of the 2300 Claim
Information loop. In the transaction set structure, these uses of the `DTP` segment occur
essentially in the same position, matching two possible occurrences of a repeating DTP segment in
the base X12 standard. Because the two uses of the segment supply different information, the HIPAA
standard gives them different names and interprets the `DTP03` field in different ways.

In this case, the data value in the first field of the segment, the `Date Time Qualifier` field,
identifies which variation of the segment is actually being used. Since the code set for this field
has different values for each of these uses, the value present in the field tells whether the DTP
segment in a parsed document is a `DTP_DateAccident` or a `DTP_DateAcuteManifestation` (or any of
several other uses of the DTP segment in the same position). The `varValue: true` flag in the schema
definition indicates that this first field is used in this manner to distinguish between variations.

Even though the value of this field is effectively fixed for each use of the segment, you must
specify it when writing data. If you supply a different value for this field, or donât supply a
value, youâll see an error when writing.

## Using Syntax Rules

The following is a final example from the `005010X222A2` `837` transaction set schema, illustrating
how syntax rules are represented:

```yaml
- id: "N4_PayerCityStateZIPCode"
  name: "Payer City, State, ZIP Code"
  varTag: "N4"
  values:
    - { id: "19", name: "Payer City Name", usage: M, type: AN, minLength: 2, maxLength: 30 }
    - { id: "156", name: "Payer State or Province Code", usage: O, type: ID, length: 2 }
    - {
        id: "116",
        name: "Payer Postal Zone or ZIP Code",
        usage: O,
        type: ID,
        minLength: 3,
        maxLength: 15,
      }
    - { id: "26", name: "Country Code", usage: O, type: ID, minLength: 2, maxLength: 3 }
    - { id: "309", name: "Location Qualifier", usage: U, type: ID, minLength: 1, maxLength: 2 }
    - { id: "310", name: "Location Identifier", usage: U, type: AN, minLength: 1, maxLength: 30 }
    - {
        id: "1715",
        name: "Country Subdivision Code",
        usage: O,
        type: ID,
        minLength: 1,
        maxLength: 3,
      }
  rules:
    - { type: E, items: [2, 7] }
    - { type: C, items: [6, 5] }
    - { type: C, items: [7, 4] }
```

Syntax rules are used in X12 and HIPAA to define relationships between values within a segment or
composite. The rules are included in the schema at the same level as the list of values. The code
for the type of rule is the same as used by X12 and HIPAA specifications, and the list of items
gives the numbers of the values governed by the rule.

In the case of the previous example, the three rules say that:

- Only one of `N402` or `N407` can be present (`{ type: E, items: [2, 7] }`)
- If `N406` is present, then `N405` is required (`{ type: C, items: [6, 5] }`)
- If `N407` is present, then `N404` is required (`{ type: C, items: [7, 4] }`)

## Modifying Schemas

Due to the differences between standard X12 and HIPAA schemas the use of overlay schemas to modify a
base definition is not supported for HIPAA. Instead, for modifications, extract the supplied HIPAA
schema from inside the `x12-schemas-2.0.0.jar` file. This is found in the standard MuleSoft
enterprise Maven repositories, under group ID com.mulesoft.connectors. You can copy a message
structure schema from this JAR file (it contains both standard X12 and HIPAA schemas) and modify the
extracted schema to use it directly. Unlike X12 schemas that use a base set of segment, composite,
and element definitions, the HIPAA schemas are self-contained. This makes it easy to make changes to
the schema without working with multiple files.

## Recommended Types of Validations from WEDI

There are two types of validations to use for integrity testing.

- Type 1: EDI syntax integrity testing

Testing of the EDI file for valid segments, segment order, element attributes, testing for numeric
values in numeric data elements, validation of X12 or NCPDP syntax, and compliance with X12 and
NCPDP rules. This validates the basic syntactical integrity of the EDI submission.

- Type 2: HIPAA syntactical requirement testing

Testing for HIPAA Implementation Guide-specific syntax requirements, such as limits on repeat
counts, used and not used qualifiers, codes, elements, and segments. Also included in this type is
testing for HIPAA required or intra-segment situational data elements, testing for non-medical code
sets as laid out in the WEDI SNIP implementation guide, and values and codes noted in the WEDI SNIP
implementation guide using an X12 code list or table.

Because the connector cannot determine a course of action for intrasegment situational data
elements, intrasegment situational data elements are not part of X12 EDI Connector and must be set
in validation logic outside of the connector.

## See Also

- [Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)
- [Use Exchange to Discover Connectors, Templates, and Examples](../../connectors/introduction/intro-use-exchange)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# X12 EDI Connector 2.18 Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## Default Configuration

### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Name</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Name for this configuration. Connectors reference the configuration with this name.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Expiration Policy</p></td><td><div><div><p>ExpirationPolicy</p></div></div></td><td><p>Configures the minimum amount of time that a dynamic configuration instance can remain idle before Mule considers it eligible for expiration. This does not mean that the instance expires at the exact moment that it becomes eligible. Mule purges the instances when appropriate.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Mule ID qualifier</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>ID qualifier for Mule in the exchange (ISA) segment. This value is used in combination with the Self Identification Interchange ID value, and if one is set, the other must also be set.<br><br>If this value is set, it affects both send and receive message handling:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On the receive side, the ISA Interchange Receiver ID Qualifier must match this value.</p></li><li><p>On the send side, this value is used as the default ISA Interchange Sender ID Qualifier when it is not set in the message data.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Mule interchange ID</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>ID for Mule in the exchange (ISA) segment. This value is used in combination with the Self Identification Interchange ID qualifier value, and if one is set the other must also be set.<br><br>If this value is set, it affects both send and receive message handling:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On the receive side, the ISA Interchange Receiver ID must match this value.</p></li><li><p>On the send side, this value is used as the default ISA Interchange Sender ID when it is not set in the message data.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Mule application code</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Application Code for Mule in the group (GS segment).<br><br>If this value is set, it affects both send and receive message handling:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On the receive side, the GS Application Receiverâs Code must match this value.</p></li><li><p>On the send side, this value is used as the default GS Application Senderâs Code when it is not set in the message data.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Partner ID qualifier</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>ID qualifier for the partner in the exchange (ISA segment). This value is used in combination with the Partner Identification Interchange ID value, and if one is set the other must also be set.<br><br>If this value is set, it affects both send and receive message handling:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On the receive side, the ISA Interchange Sender ID Qualifier must match this value.</p></li><li><p>On the send side, this value is used as the default ISA Interchange Receiver ID Qualifier when it is not set in the message data.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Partner interchange ID</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>ID for the partner in the exchange (ISA segment). This value is used in combination with the Partner Identification Interchange ID qualifier value, and if one is set the other must also be set.<br><br>If this value is set, it affects both send and receive message handling:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On the receive side, the ISA Interchange Sender ID must match this value.</p></li><li><p>On the send side, this value is used as the default ISA Interchange Receiver ID when it is not set in the message data.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Partner application code</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Application Code for the partner in the group (GS segment).<br><br>If this value is set, it affects both send and receive message handling:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On the receive side, the GS Application Senderâs Code must match this value.</p></li><li><p>On the send side, this value is used as the default GS Application Receiverâs Code when it is not set in the message data.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Form and validation</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>X12</p></li><li><p>HIPAA_SNIP1</p></li><li><p>HIPAA_SNIP2</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Schema form and validation level. Schemas used in the configuration must match the selected form. SNIP Type 1 and SNIP Type 2 validation applies to HIPAA schemas only.</p></td><td><p>X12</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Schema definitions</p></td><td><div><div><p>Array of String</p></div></div></td><td><p>List of paths for the schemas that the connector uses. The paths can be file systems or classpaths.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Character encoding</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>ASCII</p></li><li><p>IBM1047</p></li><li><p>ISO8859_1</p></li><li><p>PLATFORM</p></li><li><p>UTF8</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Character encoding for messages. This character encoding is always used for both send and receive messages.</p></td><td><p>PLATFORM</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Character set</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:<br><br><strong>BASIC </strong>EXTENDED ** UNRESTRICTED</p></div></div></td><td><p>Characters allowed in string data. Invalid characters are replaced by the substitution character, if specified, or rejected as errors if no substitution character is set or enabled for receive messages in parser options. Either way, the invalid characters are logged.</p><p>For receive messages, the invalid characters are reported in 997 functional acknowledgments.</p></td><td><p>UNRESTRICTED</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Substitution character</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Substitution character used to replace invalid characters in string values.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Version identifier suffix</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Version identifier code suffix for the group (GS segment).<br><br>If this value is set, it affects both send and receive message handling:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>On the receive side, the GS Version, Release, and Industry Identifier Code must consist of the X12 schema version (004010, 005010, and so forth) followed by this suffix. If this value is not set, the receive only verifies that the GS version identifier code starts with the configured schema version.</p></li><li><p>On the send side, the combination of the X12 schema version and this suffix is used as the GS version identifier code.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Timezone offset</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Timezone offset from the UTC to apply to the date and time elements in the Interchange Header (ISA) segment. This field accepts only values compliant with ISO 8601, which are UTC-based; for example, <code>-01:00</code>, <code>+0100</code>, <code>-0130</code>, and so on.<br><br>If you donât specify a value for this field, the ISA segment date and time is set to the local date and time of Mule runtime.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enforce length limits</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce minimum and maximum lengths for parser and writer values:<br><br>For parser values:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector rejects transactions with values that are too long or too short.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector uses the values and does not reject the transactions.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>In either case the error is logged and reported in 997/999 acknowledgments.<br><br>For writer values:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector rejects a transaction that has values that are too long or too short.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector uses the values and does not reject the transaction.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enforce character set</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce the allowed character sets:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector rejects transactions that have invalid characters.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the characters are either passed through or substituted, and the connector does not reject the transactions.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>In either case, the error is reported in 997/999 acknowledgments.</p></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enforce value repeats</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce repetition count limits for receive values:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector rejects transactions that have values repeated too many or too few times.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector accepts the values and does not reject the transactions.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>In either case the error is reported in 997/999 acknowledgments.</p></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Allows unknown segments</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to allow unknown segments in a transaction set:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector ignores the unknown segments and does not reject the transaction.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector rejects a transaction that contains unknown segments.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>In either case, the error is reported in 997/999 acknowledgments.</p></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enforce segment order</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce the segment order in a transaction set:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector rejects transactions that have out-of-order segments.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, and the connector reorders the segments and does not reject the transactions.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>In either case the error is reported in 997/999 acknowledgments.</p></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Allow unused segments</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to allow segments marked as <code>Unused</code> in a transaction set:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector ignores the unused segments and does not reject the transactions.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector rejects transactions that contain segments marked as unused in the schema.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>In either case, the error is reported in 997/999 acknowledgments.</p></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enforce segment repeats</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce segment repetition count limits in transaction sets.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector rejects transactions that repeat the segment too many times.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector ignores the repeated segments and does not reject the transactions.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>In either case the error is reported in 997/999 acknowledgments.</p></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Require unique interchanges</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to require unique Interchange Control Numbers (ISA13) for received interchanges:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector records the previously processed interchange numbers and rejects duplicate interchange numbers from the same partner (as determined by the interchange sender and receiver identification).</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector continues processing the received interchange numbers and leaves it to the application flow to track interchange numbers and handle them appropriately.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Require unique groups</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce globally unique Group Control Numbers (GS06) for received functional groups:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, group numbers must be unique across all interchanges received from the same partner and application (as determined by the interchange sender and receiver identification, combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes).</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, group numbers must be unique within an interchange.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Require unique transactions</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce globally unique Transaction Set Control Numbers (ST02) for received transaction sets:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, transaction set numbers must be unique across all functional groups received from the same partner and application (as determined by the interchange sender and receiver identification, combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes).</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, transaction set control numbers must be unique within a particular functional group.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Require unique transactions in group</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce globally unique transaction set control numbers (ST02) for received transaction sets that are scoped within the functional group:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, transaction set control numbers must be unique within the functional groups that are received from the same partner and application. For partners, the uniqueness is determined by the interchange sender and receiver identification. For the application, the uniqueness is determined by the application codes of the functional group sender and receiver.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, a functional group can have duplicate transaction set control numbers, and each instance is treated as a separate transaction set.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Store time-to-live</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Minimum number of days to store interchange, group, and transaction set numbers for checking uniqueness. This value is used only when the configuration requires unique interchange, group, or transaction set numbers.<br><br>The default persistent object store from the Mule configuration is always used for storing the received identifier numbers. This parameter sets the entry time-to-live for these stored values.</p></td><td><p>30</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledge every transaction</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to include a separate AK2/AK5 (997) or AK2/IK5 (999) acknowledgment for every received transaction set:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector explicitly acknowledges each received transaction set.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector includes in the acknowledgement only transaction sets that contains errors. Nonincluded transaction sets are implicitly acknowledged.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Generate 999 acknowledgments</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to generate 999 Implementation Acknowledgments rather than 997 Functional Acknowledgments:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, The connector generates 999 Implementation Acknowledgments for each received interchange instead of 997 Functional Acknowledgement transaction sets. Support for 999 Implementation Acknowledgments does not include CTX segment generation.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector generates 997 Functional Acknowledgment transaction sets for each received interchange.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Report segment details</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether Report segment error details to the sender in 997/999 Functional Acknowledgements:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the details of segment errors are included in a generated 997/999.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the details are not included.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Include Acknowledgment schema</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to expect 997 or 999 Functional Acknowledgments and to include the 997 or 999 schema:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector automatically includes the schema for 997 or 999 acknowledgment transaction sets in the set of schemas used by X12 Connector.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, you must directly specify the 997 schema, 999 schema, or both if you want these to be processed as input. The schemas used for generating 997 or 999 Functional Acknowledgments are hardcoded and cannot be modified.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Data element separator</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Data element separator character. The configured value is used by default for all output messages, but can be overridden at the message level.</p></td><td><p>*</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Component element separator</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Component element separator character. The configured value is used by default for all output messages, but can be overridden at the message level.</p></td><td><p>&gt;</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Repetition separator</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Repetition separator character (if used). The configured value is used by default for all output messages, but can be overridden at the message level. The value <code>U</code> means repetitions are not used.</p></td><td><p>U</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Segment terminator</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Segment terminator character. The configured value is used by default for all output messages, but can be overridden at the message level.</p></td><td><p>~</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Segment line ending</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NONE</p></li><li><p>LF</p></li><li><p>CRLF</p></li><li><p>CR</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Line ending to add between segments to improve the readability of the output message text.</p></td><td><p>NONE</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Unique group numbers</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to send globally unique Group Control Numbers (GS06):</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector assigns unique group numbers across all interchanges sent to the same partner and application (as determined by the interchange sender and receiver identification, combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes).</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector assigns functional group control numbers sequentially within each interchange and reuses the numbers in different interchanges.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Unique transaction numbers</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to send globally unique Transaction Set Control Numbers (ST02):</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector assigns unique transaction set numbers across all interchanges sent to the same partner and application (as determined by the interchange sender and receiver identification, combined with the functional group sender and receiver application codes).</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector assigns transaction set control numbers sequentially within each functional group and reuses the control numbers in different groups.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Implementation reference</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Implementation convention reference for transactions (ST03). If this value is set, it is used as the ST03 Implementation Convention Reference unless it is overridden in the message parameters.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Initial interchange number</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Initial Interchange Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td><td><p>1</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Initial group number</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Initial Group Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td><td><p>1</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Initial transaction number</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Initial Transaction Set Control Number used for outgoing messages.</p></td><td><p>1</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Requests acknowledgments</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to request acknowledgments such as TA1 for the sent transactions flag (ISA14):</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, TA1 acknowledgments are requested for all sent transactions.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, TA1 acknowledgements are not requested.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Default usage indicator</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Default interchange usage indicator (ISA15):</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>I</code>: Information</p></li><li><p><code>P</code>: Production data</p></li><li><p><code>T</code>: Test data</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>P</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Set acknowledgement schema by inbound message</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>If the <strong>acknowledgmentSchemaPath</strong> is <code>EMPTY</code> and this property is <code>true</code> it will use the <strong>Version / Release / Industry Identifier Code (GS08)</strong> field value from the inbound message to set the Functional/Implementation Acknowledgment structure. When <code>false</code> it uses a hardcoded Functional/Implementation Acknowledgment structure of the 005010 version.</p></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledgement schema path</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The path from the file system or the <code>classpath</code>. If the path is specified, it overwrites the default acknowledgment path.</p></div></div></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enforce conditional rules</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to apply conditional rules in transactions:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector rejects transactions with values that donât meet conditional rules.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the values are used and the transaction is not rejected.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Truncate values exceeding the length limits</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to truncate values that exceed the length limits when the <strong>Enforce length limits</strong> field is <code>true</code>:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector trims the values that exceed the maximum length limit.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector writes the values as-is.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enforce code set validations</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to enforce code set validations:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, the connector enforces code set validations, as defined in the X12 schemas.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, the connector does not enforce code set validations.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Use segment line ending in last line</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to use the configured line ending in the last line of the IEA segment:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, adds the configured <strong>Segment line ending</strong> field value to the end of the IEA segment.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, does not add the configured <strong>Segment line ending</strong> value to the IEA segment.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Report non-fatal errors in acknowledgments</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to report non-fatal errors in acknowledgements:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, non-fatal errors are displayed at segments IK3/AK3 in acknowledgments.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, non-fatal errors are not displayed at segments IK3/AK3 in acknowledgments.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Include trailer segment details</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><div><div><p>Whether to include interchange and group trailer details in the map:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If <code>true</code>, interchange trailer, group trailer, and set trailer details are included in each transaction.</p></li><li><p>If <code>false</code>, only set trailer details are included in each transaction.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Lock Mechanism</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>AUTO</p></li><li><p>RUNTIME_LOCK_MANAGER</p></li><li><p>OBJECT_STORE</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the mechanism used to manage locks for control number sequencing:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>AUTO</code></p><div><p>The connector chooses between <code>OBJECT_STORE</code> and <code>RUNTIME_LOCK_MANAGER</code> based on the environment in which the Mule app is running.</p></div><div><ul><li><p>When the app is deployed to CloudHub 1.0, the connector uses the <code>OBJECT_STORE</code> mechanism for lock management.</p></li><li><p>When the app is deployed to stand-alone Mule runtime, Runtime Fabric, or CloudHub 2.0, the connector uses the <a href="../../mule-runtime/latest/distributed-locking">runtimeâs distributed locking</a>.</p></li></ul></div><div><p>You can use this option with:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>CloudHub 1.0 deployments, including multiple workers or replicas</p></li><li><p>CloudHub 2.0 and Runtime Fabric deployments with one worker or replica</p></li><li><p>Hybrid standalone deployments, including clustered Mule runtimes</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p><code>RUNTIME_LOCK_MANAGER</code></p><div><p>The connector always uses the runtimeâs distributed locking mechanism.
